data.title: Why a Coding Agent May Emerge as a Key Unlock for Growth
data.description:
Investing.com - Snowflake Inc (NYSE:SNOW) is doubling down on its "Cortex" AI suite, launching a native coding agent designed to streamline data integration and accelerate revenue realization. According to a new research note from Morgan Stanley (NYSE: MS), the rollout of Cortex Code marks a strategic pivot toward improving the developer experience. 
[[pro_promotion |
Get premium news and insight, AI stock picks, and deep research tools by upgrading to InvestingPro
]]
By automating the generation and optimization of SQL and Python code, Snowflake aims to reduce migration timelines and move data into its Cloud Data Platform more efficiently, ultimately serving as a catalyst for increased query volumes.
Cortex code and the “data unlock” strategy
Unlike general-purpose AI coding assistants, Cortex Code is specifically tailored to the Snowflake environment. The tool enables users to automate data pipelines and build data applications directly on top of Snowflake data with reduced manual intervention. 
Analysts suggest that this native integration is a key unlock for growth, as it addresses the primary bottleneck for many enterprise clients: the time and technical complexity required to migrate legacy data. 
Recent feedback from global partners indicates a healthy spend environment, with Cortex Code already beginning to impact customer deployments.
The pace of innovation at the data-warehousing giant has remained rapid, with several major announcements, including Snowflake Intelligence and SnowWork, hitting the market in recent months. 
Morgan Stanley remains "Overweight" on the stock, citing a substantial runway for further migrations and the anticipated full-year contribution from these new AI-driven product cycles. 
The goal is to move from data ingestion to revenue-generating queries at a faster clip, providing a structural tailwind for the company’s consumption-based business model.
Competitive positioning in the software stack
The move into coding agents follows a broader trend among high-growth software firms to integrate generative AI as a core functional layer rather than a standalone feature. By focusing on the developer experience, Snowflake is positioning itself to capture a larger share of the enterprise data stack, even as competition in the cloud sector intensifies. 
Investors are closely monitoring how these innovations translate into realized revenue, particularly as customers seek to optimize their cloud spend while expanding their AI capabilities.
As long as the company can maintain this cadence of product releases, analysts believe it is well-positioned to outperform in a robust software spending environment. 
The primary metric for the remainder of 2026 will be the conversion rate of these "coding agent" deployments into sustained query growth, which remains the fundamental driver of Snowflake’s long-term valuation.

data.title: Australian states pivot to free public transport as fuel shortages rattle economy
data.description:
Investing.com - State governments in Victoria and Tasmania have announced emergency measures to waive public transport fares, aiming to insulate households from surging fuel costs. The move follows a month of heightened supply risks in the Middle East that have begun to impact Australia’s domestic energy security. 
[[pro_promotion |
Get deeper insights on how the Iran war impacts global markets - InvestingPro
]]
Victoria will offer one month of free travel starting March 31, while Tasmania has opted for a more extended reprieve, removing costs for buses and ferries from March 30 through July 1.
Supply disruptions and the RBA’s inflation dilemma
The relief measures come as the country faces rare domestic shortages, with hundreds of service stations reporting fuel shortfalls. These disruptions are starting to bleed into the nation’s industrial core, with the agriculture and mining sectors reporting delivery delays. 
Although Prime Minister Anthony Albanese recently moved to reassure markets that near-term supply remains secure, the federal government is expected to introduce new powers in Parliament this Monday to underwrite private sector fuel purchases from international markets.
The Reserve Bank of Australia (RBA) sees these energy-led price pressures as complicating an already hawkish policy path. Having recently raised the key cash rate to 4.1% in a split board decision, the central bank now faces a synchronized shock of rising transport costs and pre-existing inflation. 
Market traders have reacted by fully pricing in two additional rate hikes before the end of the year, as the fuel crunch threatens to unanchor inflation expectations across the broader economy.
Federal intervention and resource sector exposure
The federal government’s plan to shore up fuel security marks a significant shift in interventionist policy. By underwriting private sector energy imports, the administration hopes to stabilize the supply chain for critical resource sectors. 
However, until the latest measures are ratified and global maritime routes stabilize, the higher operational costs for mining and freight remain a primary concern for regional growth. 
Investors are closely watching the Monday parliamentary session for further details on the scope of the government’s emergency energy mandates.

data.title: How will higher freight costs impact off-price retailers?
data.description:
Investing.com - Major off-price retailers are better positioned to weather the current spike in logistics costs than during previous supply chain crises, thanks to strategic price increases and a more stable economic outlook.
[[pro_promotion |
Get more insights by upgrading to InvestingPro - up to 50% discount now
]] 
According to a new industry analysis from Bank of America Corp (NYSE:BAC), players like TJX Companies Inc (NYSE:TJX), Ross Stores Inc (NASDAQ:ROST), and Burlington Stores Inc (NYSE:BURL) are utilizing higher Average Unit Retail (AUR) gains to offset margin pressure. 
The price adjustments allow retailers to ship and distribute fewer units for every dollar of sales, creating a structural hedge against rising transport overheads.
Diesel surges and the 2H margin outlook
The current environment remains challenging, but the scale of the pressure is significantly lower than the disruptions seen in 2021/22. Ocean freight rates are currently up 8%, a stark contrast to the 250% surge seen four years ago. 
However, diesel prices have climbed 50% year-over-year to $5.38 per gallon. The spike is expected to hit margins almost immediately through domestic fuel surcharges. 
Analysts estimate this could result in approximately 20 basis points of gross margin pressure for TJX, far below the 280-basis-point peak hit in late 2022.
The full impact of higher ocean contracts is expected to materialize in the second half of the year as new inventory, negotiated at current higher rates, hits store shelves. Despite the headwinds, the sector remains a favorite for analysts, who expect "trade-down" behavior from budget-conscious consumers to continue driving market share gains. 
Higher sales growth and expense favorability are anticipated to act as further stabilizers for the group’s bottom line through the remainder of the 2026 fiscal year.
Inventory discipline and market share gains
The resilience of the off-price model is further bolstered by disciplined inventory management. By focusing on higher-margin units, such retailers are effectively lowering their "freight intensity." 
As long as consumer demand for discounted name-brand apparel remains robust, the sector is likely to outperform broader specialty retail. 
Investors are closely monitoring the upcoming annual ocean contract negotiations, which will serve as the primary indicator for margin stability heading into the critical fourth-quarter holiday season.

data.title: U.S. and India remain split on WTO e-commerce moratorium extension
data.description:
Investing.com - Trade ministers gathered in Cameroon for the World Trade Organization (WTO) summit have entered the final day of negotiations with no breakthrough on a critical moratorium regarding digital customs duties. 
[[pro_promotion |
Get premium news and insight, AI stock picks, and deep research tools by upgrading to InvestingPro
]]
The primary point of contention remains the extension of a long-standing ban on imposing tariffs on electronic transmissions, such as digital downloads and software. 
The current moratorium is set to expire this month, and the outcome of the talks is being viewed by many as a definitive test of the WTO’s relevance following a year of global trade turmoil and supply chain disruptions.
The standoff: Permanence vs. temporary extension
The negotiations are currently stalled by a significant gap between the positions of the United States and India. U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer has signaled that Washington is not interested in a temporary renewal, instead pushing for a permanent ban to ensure long-term market predictability. 
Conversely, Indian diplomats have indicated a willingness to accept only a two-year extension. While some Western diplomats are exploring a "pathway to permanence", potentially a 10-year compromise, there is little consensus among the broader membership to go beyond the 24-month mark.
Business leaders have warned that a failure to extend the moratorium would invite a wave of new duties, stripping away the regulatory certainty that has underpinned the digital economy for decades. 
U.S. officials have further cautioned that a lapse in the agreement could diminish Washington’s engagement with the trade body, with U.S. Ambassador Joseph Barloon noting that a permanent extension is vital for maintaining U.S. confidence in the organization’s effectiveness.
Institutional reforms and subsidy transparency
The e-commerce debate is unfolding alongside broader efforts to overhaul WTO rules, including making subsidy use more transparent and streamlining the consensus-based decision-making process. 
The U.S. and the European Union continue to argue that current frameworks, such as the "Most-Favored-Nation" principle, have been leveraged by China to their detriment. 
However, the reform efforts face stiff opposition from a handful of members who are blocking a detailed work plan, stating that such changes could erode the body’s founding principles.
As the clock runs down in Yaoundé, the focus for markets is on whether the WTO can avoid a lame duck scenario. 
A failure to secure the e-commerce moratorium would not only trigger immediate concerns over digital trade costs but could also signal a deeper fragmentation of the rules-based international trading system going forward.

data.title: Baltic port attacks halt Russian crude loadings as Ukraine targets energy hubs
data.description:
Investing.com - Russia’s crude export capabilities have suffered a major setback following a fresh wave of Ukrainian drone attacks on the Ust-Luga port. According to Leningrad Governor Alexander Drozdenko, air defenses intercepted 31 drones overnight, but emergency services are currently battling a significant fire at the terminal. 
[[pro_promotion |
Get premium commodity markets insights with analyst comments on InvestingPro
]]
The latest strike marks a serious escalation in Kyiv’s strategy to target energy infrastructure, effectively halting loadings at one of Russia’s most critical maritime gateways since last Wednesday.
Baltic hubs offline and export volume at risk
The sustained campaign against the Leningrad region has now impacted both the Ust-Luga and Primorsk ports, the two primary outlets for Russian oil on the Baltic coast. Before the disruptions, the two facilities collectively handled approximately 45% of Russia’s seaborne crude exports, totaling 1.72 million barrels per day. 
With both hubs sustaining significant damage, a massive portion of the nation’s energy logistics is now effectively sidelined, creating a bottleneck that threatens to stifle global supply at a time of heightened market sensitivity.
The timing of these strikes is particularly damaging for the Kremlin’s fiscal outlook. Global oil price increases, driven by the ongoing conflict in the Middle East, had initially offered a windfall to cover a widening budget gap.
The inability to move physical barrels significantly blunts that potential revenue boost. As the war enters its fifth year, the shift toward targeting economic infrastructure suggests a transition toward a war of industrial attrition, where the ability to fund military operations is directly linked to the integrity of energy terminals.
Retaliatory strikes and the economic ceiling
The conflict continues to see daily exchanges of drone and missile fire, with Russia targeting Ukrainian industrial and civilian sites in regions such as Mykolaiv. However, the precision of the strikes on Baltic hubs represents a structural challenge to the Russian economy that is difficult to mitigate through air defense alone. 
Investors’ focus remains on the duration of the repairs at the two ports. Any prolonged outage at Ust-Luga and Primorsk will likely force a redirection of global crude flows and keep a firm floor under international oil benchmarks.

data.title: AI data center networking boom gathers pace: Here are the best stocks to watch
data.description: Analysts at Bernstein have highlighted the AI data center (AIDC) networking space as one set to evolve into one of the fastest-growing domains that could last multiple years, even as it remains central to system-level efficiency of modern AI workloads. Against this backdrop, there are several stocks well-positioned to benefit.The total addressable market (TAM) for AIDC networking chips is estimated to reach around $100 billion in 2030 with around 30% CAGR, notes Bernstein.“As AI model sizes and computational requirements scale exponentially, single chips are no longer sufficient. Modern AI workloads demand massive clusters of accelerators that must operate as a unified computing fabric,” analysts at the firm explained in a note to clients.The reason demand is exploding is the compound bandwidth effect, as accelerators in multi-tier networking structures increase point-to-point bandwidth while also multiplying traffic across higher tiers of the cluster. This increases the requirement for more networking components and layers of connections once the number of chips crosses a certain level.“As hyperscalers accelerate the build out of 100k+ GPU clusters, networking components become the second-largest cost, positioning the AIDC networking TAM to grow faster than xPUs,” the analysts added.There are three major connection types: DC-DC connections, CPU-centric connections, and xPU-to-xPU connections. Within xPU-to-xPU, there are scale-up and scale-out networks. Each of these connects different parts of the cluster, giving rise to different protocols, “each tailored to trade-offs in bandwidth, latency, cost.”These include Nvidia’s (NVDA) NVLink, PCIe, Ethernet, UALink, and China’s UB.“Competition in the scale-up networking domain remains intense and far from settled,” said Bernstein.While Nvidia’s NVLink continues to serve as the performance benchmark, given its “tight hardware-software integration and proven performance,” it’s a closed system and alternatives are gaining traction.Among these, UALink and Ethernet-based SUE architectures promote open ecosystems, reduce vendor lock and lower costs.PCIe is favoured among cloud service providers looking for “mature, inexpensive solutions to certain workloads.”As for China, the firm notes the country could follow a “distinct path” as Huawei’s Unified Bus is designed as a unified architecture across multiple layers. This reflects a strategic focus on bigger cluster scale.Given the technological and capital barriers in high performance interconnects, the analysts note that this limits new entrants and keeps margins high.Companies that stand to gain as a result are Nvidia, Huawei, Broadcom (AVGO), Marvell Technology (MRVL), Montage and other vendors in China.Nvidia and Huawei both offer a “closed ecosystem that benefits from a fully integrated end-to-end fabric across computing and networking.”As for AVGO and MRVL, they benefit from CSP adoption of open ecosystems like Ethernet.NVDA is rated Outperform with a price target of $300; AVGO is also rated Outperform with a price target of $525.

data.title: Barclays warns ‘Trump put’ losing impact amid policy swings
data.description:
Investing.com - Barclays told clients in a note on Friday that recent market resilience driven by de-escalation signals from U.S. President Donald Trump may be fading as policy uncertainty intensifies. 
[[pro_promotion |
Access more analyst research and stock-level insights on InvestingPro
]]
Analyst Emmanuel Cau wrote that “constant flip-flopping and headline fatigue is starting to undermine the put efficacy”.
Cau noted that equities have so far been supported by what he called Trump’s “apparent willingness to de-escalate the conflict,” adding that markets reacted sharply to the shifting deadlines the president set for Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz. 
“Panic in oil, rates and equities was palpable when markets opened on Monday, before Trump extended to Friday the 48h ultimatum,” he wrote, noting the deadline was later pushed again after another day of stress.
Barclays stated that the situation “remains fluid and rather confusing” as Israel intensifies strikes and the United States reportedly sends more troops to the Middle East. 
While hedge fund and CTA de-grossing may help tactical positioning, Cau commented that resilient price action indicates the “market wants to go up.” 
He added that long-only investors “remain in the glass half-full camp,” leaving the risk of a prolonged conflict “under priced.”
Barclays warned that the longer the conflict and related oil shock persist, the stronger the threat of stagflation. 
“Our economists now expect global growth in % Q4/Q4 2026 terms to be 2.9%, while global inflation will get pushed higher to 2.7% by end-2026. Advanced economies in aggregate are expected to grow only 1.7% 2026 (all Q4/Q4), driven by weaker growth in the euro area (0.7%), the UK (1.0%) and Japan (1.4%),” concluded Cau.

data.title: Expert says the upcoming week will be a pivotal moment in the Iran war. Here’s why
data.description:
Investing.com - The war between the United States, Israel, and Iran is entering a critical decision window that will determine whether the region moves toward a narrow diplomatic settlement or a significant military escalation. According to a high-level briefing from Jefferies (NYSE: JEF) featuring former intelligence and State Department officials, the coming days represent a pivotal moment for the month-long conflict. 
[[pro_promotion |
Get deeper insights on how the Iran war impacts global markets - InvestingPro
]]
Analysts suggest that while Iran’s military capacity has been weakened, it remains far from neutralized, leaving the path forward heavily dependent on the next moves from the White House.
Narrow paths to de-escalation
Strategic experts indicate that any potential off-ramp for the conflict is likely to be extremely narrow. President Donald Trump has signaled a willingness to negotiate, but there is a growing consensus that a comprehensive, durable peace deal remains unrealistic in the near term. 
Instead, the markets are monitoring the possibility of a limited arrangement centered on the reopening of vital maritime corridors. Should the diplomatic efforts fail to gain traction within the current window, the risk of the conflict expanding to include broader regional infrastructure remains high.
Market participants view U.S. leadership as the primary factor in preventing a total breakdown of regional trade. The experts noted that the Iranian leadership is currently navigating a period of intense internal pressure, which could either force a tactical retreat or trigger a more desperate retaliatory cycle. 
The current geopolitical uncertainty has kept a persistent risk premium on global energy benchmarks, as investors weigh the likelihood of a "managed" exit against the potential for a wider, more destructive engagement.
Strategic outlook and tactical risks
As the April 6 deadline for strikes on Iranian power facilities approaches, the focus is shifting toward the resilience of regional defenses. Although several high-value targets have been degraded, Iran maintains the ability to disrupt global shipping and strike at Gulf energy nodes. 
The baseline expectation for the markets is one of continued volatility, with the U.S. dollar remaining a favored haven as long as the threat of escalation looms over the Persian Gulf.

data.title: Leading broker sees gold falling to $3,700 as a real possibility
data.description:
Investing.com - Gold’s recent pullback may have further to run, with technical and macro signals pointing to a prolonged consolidation phase rather than a quick rebound, according to technical strategists at Bank of America. 
XAU/USD rose 2.6% yesterday, with Gold Futures also gaining, though they are now up less than 4% year to date.
[[pro_promotion |
Upgrade to InvestingPro to read more on gold outlook - up to 50% off now
]]




Why is gold falling?
Macro forces are weighing on bullion. BCA strategist Peter Berezin pointed to three main drivers behind gold’s recent weakness. First, the U.S. dollar has strengthened while interest rate expectations have moved higher.
“From a macro perspective, a stronger greenback and higher rates are usually bad news for gold,” Berezin said.
Second, positioning played a role. Gold—and especially silver—entered March in an overbought state. In such conditions, risk-off episodes can trigger sharp declines as leveraged or short-term investors exit positions. Berezin noted that similar dynamics were seen in past episodes, including in October 2008, when gold dropped sharply despite broader market stress.
Third, official sector demand appears to be shifting. Some central banks are scaling back purchases or even selling reserves. Poland is reportedly considering gold sales to fund defense spending, while Turkey has been selling gold to support its currency. There are also signs that some Gulf states may be slowing purchases amid weaker export revenues.
Taken together, the combination of technical consolidation, tighter financial conditions, and softer central bank demand suggests gold may remain under pressure in the coming quarters, even after its strong multi-year run.
BofA sees more downside risk


 

Strategist Paul Ciana said the yellow metal appears to be in a corrective “wave-four” phase, a pattern that typically follows a strong rally and can last for several months.
“Price patterns and momentum signals indicate that gold remains in a wave-four consolidation phase,” he wrote, adding that the structure “can reasonably persist through Q2 and into Q3.”
That suggests gold may struggle to regain its previous highs in the near term. The failure to sustain the January peak reinforces expectations for range-bound trading with a downside bias.
Ciana said the bank sees risk toward the $4,000 level, with the 50-week moving average—currently near $3,967—acting as a key technical reference point. Given the scale of gold’s prior rally, a deeper pullback would not be unusual, he noted
Prices surged from around $1,810 in late 2023 to nearly $6,000 in early 2026. Within that context, Ciana said “a retracement toward $3,700 would not be unusual,” describing it as consistent with a standard correction following such a strong advance.

data.title: Iran warns of attacks on American universities as U.S. prepares ground troops
data.description:
Investing.com - Geopolitical risk in the Middle East has entered a new phase after Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) issued a direct threat against U.S.-affiliated universities in neighboring nations. 
[[pro_promotion |
Get deeper insights on how the Iran war impacts global markets - InvestingPro
]]
In a statement released Sunday, the IRGC warned students, faculty, and staff to remain at least one kilometer away from the institutions, explicitly labeling them as legitimate military targets. 
The escalation follows accusations from Tehran that the U.S. and Israel were involved in a recent strike on the Tehran University of Science and Technology.
Educational infrastructure and regional presence
The threat targets a significant network of American academic "flags" planted across the region, particularly in the United Arab Emirates and Qatar. Major institutions with a high-profile presence include New York University (NYU) in Abu Dhabi, as well as Georgetown, Carnegie Mellon, and Northwestern within Qatar’s Education City. 
These U.S. campuses represent substantial long-term investments, often backed by significant financial support from host governments. According to the Open Doors project, approximately 4,900 Americans were studying in the Middle East and North Africa during the 2023-2024 academic year, with over 1,000 situated in the UAE alone.
U.S. Marines arrive in the Middle East
U.S. defense officials are preparing for the possibility of ground operations in Iran lasting several weeks, according to a Saturday report from the Washington Post. 
The arrival of specialized U.S. Marine Expeditionary Units in the Middle East, aboard the USS Tripoli, marks a significant transition toward a multi-domain military posture, moving beyond the exclusive air and sea campaigns of the past month. 
The White House has described the deployment as a contingency measure to secure energy corridors and critical infrastructure, but the move has fueled intense speculation regarding the prospect of a protracted ground war. 
Iran allows Pakistani vessels safe passage through Hormuz
Senator Ishaq Dar, the Deputy Prime Minister and Foreign Minister of Pakistan, announced that the Government of Iran has agreed to allow 20 additional ships under the Pakistani flag to transit the Strait of Hormuz. 
Under the terms of the agreement, two ships will be permitted to cross the strategic waterway daily. The development offers a targeted logistical reprieve for Pakistani maritime trade amid the ongoing regional naval restrictions.
The Deputy Prime Minister characterized the announcement as a constructive gesture and a vital confidence-building measure for regional stability. Senator Dar suggested that such diplomatic breakthroughs serve as a harbinger of peace in a volatile environment, highlighting that dialogue and diplomacy remain the only viable way forward. 
Yemen’s Houthis launch attacks against Israel 
Tensions across the region were further exacerbated by a series of Houthi-led strikes launched against Israel on Saturday. The Yemen-based group utilized a combination of drones and missiles in the attack.
The attacks highlight the continued volatility of the regional security landscape and the persistent threat to Israeli territory from multiple fronts. 
The escalation by the Houthis underscores the complexity of the ongoing conflict and the challenges facing international efforts to prevent a wider regional conflagration.
U.S.-Iran talks progress
President Trump’s top Iran envoy Steve Witkoff said the administration plans to meet with Iranian officials this week.
"We are negotiating. It’s clear. Some ships are coming through," Witkoff said at a gathering organized in part by the Saudi Arabian sovereign wealth fund. "We think there will be meetings this week. We’re certainly hopeful for it."
Witkoff said the 15-point deal, which Iran has had for "a bit of time," requires Iran to stop enriching uranium, to give up the almost 10,000 kilograms of enriched material it has, and to agree to oversight.
